items.headline: U.S. Markets Open Higher Amid Geopolitical Tensions and AI Optimism
items.summary: U.S. stock markets are rebounding at the start of the week, with major indices opening in positive territory, aiming to snap a three-week losing streak for the S&P 500. Despite the positive open, overall market sentiment remains mixed, influenced by ongoing geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East and persistently high crude oil prices. Investors are also closely watching the Federal Reserve's upcoming interest rate decision this week and fresh economic data. Optimism around artificial intelligence (AI) innovation continues to drive gains in the technology sector.

items.headline: Meta Platforms
items.summary: Shares are up around 2.6%-3% (pre-market +2.98%) following reports of planned major AI-related job cuts to manage costs and a significant $27 billion deal with Dutch cloud provider Nebius for AI capacity.

items.headline: Micron Technology
items.summary: The stock surged about 4.4% (pre-market +5.46%) after a brokerage firm raised its price target and the company announced plans to build a second manufacturing site in Taiwan to boost advanced DRAM products supply.

items.headline: Nvidia
items.summary: Gained approximately 1% (pre-market +2.11%) as investors anticipate major announcements from its annual GTC conference today, focusing on new AI chips and platforms like the 'Vera Rubin' AI architecture.

items.headline: Nebius Group
items.summary: Experience a significant jump of +13.06% (pre-market +14.27%) due to its substantial $27 billion deal to supply Meta Platforms with dedicated AI capacity.

items.headline: Ulta Beauty Inc.
items.summary: Was a major loser on Friday, plummeting 14.2%.

items.headline: Dollar Tree
items.summary: Fell 6% after reporting mixed fourth-quarter results, with earnings beating expectations but revenue slightly missing forecasts.

items.headline: Sector Performance: Tech and Energy Lead, Consumer Discretionary Lags
items.summary: Technology and AI-related companies are currently leading the market. The energy sector has also been a top performer year-to-date due to high oil prices, with a noticeable rotation towards defensive areas like consumer staples and utilities. Conversely, consumer discretionary was the only sector experiencing an earnings decline in Q4, and it, along with Industrials and Information Technology, tumbled on Friday.

items.headline: Fed Meeting and Key Economic Data Ahead This Week
items.summary: The Federal Reserve's interest rate decision is a central focus for markets this week, with expectations for the Fed to hold rates steady at 3.50%-3.75%. Today, the U.S. is releasing industrial production data for February, anticipated to show a 0.2% increase. February's Producer Price Index (PPI) is also expected later in the week. Recent data indicated steady but sticky inflation pressures in February and January, while Q4 2025 U.S. GDP growth was revised down to 0.7%. A U.S. district judge recently blocked the Justice Department's subpoenas against the Federal Reserve and Chair Jerome Powell.

items.headline: Q4 Earnings Season Nearing End with Strong Results; Key Reports Today
items.summary: The S&P 500's Q4 earnings season is concluding with robust results, achieving 13.6% earnings growth and 9.2% revenue gains, with ten out of eleven sectors reporting growth. Today, companies such as Agenus ($AGEN), Assertio Therapeutics ($ASRT), Dollar Tree ($DLTR), Ocean Power Technologies ($OPTT), and IHS Holding Limited ($IHS) are expected to report earnings. Last week, Oracle's strong earnings fueled AI investment optimism, while Adobe saw shares drop despite beating forecasts due to a CEO departure.

items.headline: Geopolitical Tensions, Oil Prices, and AI Continue to Shape Market Landscape
items.summary: The ongoing Middle East conflict remains a significant macroeconomic factor, contributing to market uncertainty and elevated crude oil prices near $100 per barrel. Disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz are a key concern for energy supply. Artificial intelligence development continues to be a primary driver for equity markets and corporate earnings. U.S.-China trade negotiations are ongoing in Paris, potentially impacting global supply chains. The credit market is showing signs of stress with downgrades outnumbering upgrades for the fifth consecutive week and rising delinquency rates.
